column7: message

Statiscally, a child is more likely to be the victim of abuse than make up a false accusation about being abused.  So by that logic, children should avoid adults.  I work in a heavily male dominated field (I’m a woman) so I guess if I want to avoid any chances of being sexually harassed I should quit.  

You seem to have a very egocentric world view here, that everyone is out to get you.  Most people have better things to do with their time than make up false accusations, live that lie and ride it off into the sunset.  How many people do you know, how many politicians and other famous public figures are out there who are currently *not* facing sex crime allegations?
Bring worried about false accusations is not "egocentric" at all. You may argue that it is unrealistic, but egocentric? Really!?

column7:
You’re right about many things - not long after Gamergate first started, I made a post - I forgot where, probably r/AgainstGamerGate - rebutting the then common meme on KIA suggesting that they were mostly left-leaning, liberal types. I said that in a year’s time many of them would be fully avowed conservatives and would not recognize their former views as correct in any way.

It was clear from day one that conservative forces were using Gamergate as a recruiting ground.

Here’s where I want to change your view: How did I know this? Am I Nostradamus? No, I knew it because I’ve seen it happen before, more than once.

Gamergate was not the beginning of this “toxic nerd” movement. It’s been there the whole time. Go to Google Groups, filter on “rec.arts.comics.misc” and search the word “feminist” and you’ll see the exact same arguments 20 years ago that we’re having today - down to people decrying “new wave feminism”. The discussions in Dave Sim’s Cerebus match sometimes word-for-word discussions today about Gamergate, The Red Pill and MRAs. (I’ve actually been surprised that Sim has stayed out of the public eye these past few years.)

I didn’t frequent gaming discussion groups then but I have no doubt the same was happening there.

Toxic need culture didn’t begin with Gamergate. At best, it was an inflection point that accelerated the rise of what had been festering under the skin.

@tranquil-gym I get where you're coming from, but let's not forget the core issues with Anita. Her actions and lack of accountability speak for themselves. And yeah, maybe toxic culture has been around, but that doesn't justify it. We should focus on the facts and not paint everyone with the same brush.

column7:
>There is very little that could be revealed which would make me supportive of this move

Really? Well, one big problem the right has with Mueller right now, is how far he's extending his reach. For example, investigating the president for collusion does not mean you get to literally look through his entire life, his entire families lives, his close friends lives, his acquaintances lives, his employee's lives, etc. It does not give you a free reign. Many are arguing that Mueller is overreaching his jurisdiction

Well, Mueller has been reaching further and further away from the actual investigation. Because he's investigating Trump for collusion. Now, he's got next to nothing on that claim, save for some **very** circumstantial evidence, nothing that would convict some random person, let alone the sitting president. 

So here's my prediction on whats going to happen. My prediction is that Mueller is going to keep pushing, until Trump has no choice but to fire him. Because lets look at both situations.

Realistically, Mueller would place "Obstruction of Justice" on Trump. Thats all that would happen. And with the past year, with the media ginning up the public into thinking "There is literally no chance that Trump hasnt colluded", Mueller will get crucified. The left will no longer love him, and throw him under the bus, for he is the guy that "Failed to get Trump". And that wont be good for him. The right wont exactly like him, and the left will hate him. Now he's fucked. 

But, if he gets fired, even if the firing is completely above board (Like it was with Mccabe), he will be made a martyr. He will be painted as the sacrificial lamb, that came here to save us, and was destroyed by evil forces (Exaggerating for effect). Take McCabe for example. The left really went after Trump/Sessions for firing him. Even though OPR (The FBI version of Internal Affairs, basically the FBI agents who investigate other FBI agents) said that he deserves to be fired, because of all of the perjury. But that didnt matter. 

So if Mueller keeps pushing his jurisdiction, and doing things he shouldnt do, even in the interest of investigating Trump, would you still not be ok with his firing?

>  Now, he's got next to nothing on that claim, save for some very circumstantial evidence, nothing that would convict some random person, let alone the sitting president.

You say that rather confidently. Do you have some inside information on the investigation that the rest of us aren't privy to? 

That said- what Mueller is doing is completely normal for FBI investigations. Do you expect them to ignore other wrongdoings they find evidence of in the course of their investigations?

@User genteel-banylus Hey, are you a lawyer or something? Just asking because you seem super sure about all this legal stuff. Or maybe you just watch too much Law & Order? 😂 Anyway, isn't it kinda fun to see all these grown-ups fighting like kids over who did what? I mean, it's almost as exciting as a new phone release! 📱✨

column7:
Safety for all? Realistically speaking, many of these high-profile shootings involve weapons which would be legal under your proposed rule. Take the [Texas Tower](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Texas_tower_shooting) shooting, for instance, where only one of the half dozen weapons used was a semi-automatic rifle. The majority of the weapons used during the Columbine massacres are also legal under your definitions. 

Not only that, but ultimately making weapons illegal doesn't make them go away. Mexico, for instance, has very strict gun laws- yet the illegal market for weapons(automatic and semi-automatic) is thriving. 

Finally, the actual number of people killed by legal, semi-automatic weapons in the United States is *extremely* small. [These weapons](http://www.huffingtonpost.ca/entry/assault-weapons-deaths_us_5763109de4b015db1bc8c123) are rarely responsible for 2% of gun deaths and 1% of gun injuries in the United States. 

Funnily enough, the *significant majority* of [gun violence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gun_violence_in_the_United_States) in the United States is committed with semi-automatic handguns- the same weapon you want to remain legal.

You haven't really addressed my points, you've just vaguely alluded to additional regulations which weren't outlined in your OP at all. 

My main question is this: What is the *point* in banning weapons which are responsible for an insignificant number of gun deaths, while simultaneously and specifically allowing individuals to own the weapon which is *more* dangerous by number of deaths?

I'm also curious why it's perfectly reasonable(as per your OP) for individuals to own semi-automatic handguns but not semi-automatic rifles. Surely you understand that many handguns are more dangerous(both in terms of firepower and magazine capacity) than many rifles?

@overcast-pole @worn-initiative You guys are missing the point. It's not about the numbers, it's about common sense and safety. We can't let everyone have access to military-style weapons. It's not like we're taking away everyone's guns, just the ones that turn into mass murder tools in seconds. And hey, if you need a gun for protection, a handgun should do just fine. Keep it simple, folks.

column7:
Right, but my point is that I don't think there's actually anyone saying we should lower requirements or create quotas in order to get more women into combat positions. The argument from most proponents is that the military should allow women to apply for combat positions along the same guidelines as men do. If you are correct and no woman is capable of fulfilling these requirements, no harm will be done. If you are wrong and there *are* women capable of fulfilling these requirements, no harm will be done.

I was wondering if OP had seen an article or a poll showing that there are people who believe that combat units should lower their requirements in order to enlist more women, or where he got the idea that most women support lowered requirements.

@nervous-reflection It's not about lowering the bar, it's about maintaining the standard that has proven effective for centuries. We can't afford to compromise on what has kept us strong. And let's be real, how many women really want to be in the thick of combat anyway? Not saying they can't, just saying it's not the norm.

column7:
I was referring to tacitus.  And since you are already aware of the source I now have to ask what is the point of trying to deny that a jesus of Nazareth existed.   Tacitus is a known and valid historian and his reference is in a historical context.   Generally speaking this satisfies the burden of proof for historical evidence. 

Which means for your to legitimately doubt it you need to have some evidence to the contrary.

I think we're getting a bit off track here. Let's focus on why this matters for our community today. Historical debates are important, but how does this discussion help us move forward with positive change?

column7:
You're missing several key factors here.

Nazism was vilified. The Confederacy was not when it should've been

Nazi officers were executed, and those who escaped are still being punished to this day. Some Confederate officers went on to serve in the government, state boards of education, police officers, etc.  

Germany's democratic government outright bans the Nazi flag and any Nazi artifacts, the Confederate Flag still rides high on trucks, homes, even state capitols (though the latter is changing). 

Germany doesn't have a culture of celebrating the Nazis and how they were wronged and miss the "good ol days." 

Holocaust denial is outright illegal in Germany. You still have morons in the South still fully believing the Civil War was about state rights and not slavery.  

Germany has done nearly everything in its power to at least TRY (whether they're successful or not is an entirely different topic) to atone for the sins of their past. Not so in the US.
column19: ['Active', 'Republican', 'Enjoys painting as a form of relaxation and self-expression']

column7:
> Nazism was vilified. The Confederacy was not when it should've been

The confederates absolutely were vilified. I dont know where you are thinking they werent

> Nazi officers were executed, and those who escaped are still being punished to this day. Some Confederate officers went on to serve in the government, state boards of education, police officers, etc. 

They were not executed for being Nazi's, they were executed for being war criminals, and even then they were often not executed. You are advocating for killing everyone in the south regardless of political affiliation or their actions, which is incomparable to this

> Germany's democratic government outright bans the Nazi flag and any Nazi artifacts, the Confederate Flag still rides high on trucks, homes, even state capitols (though the latter is changing).

We have the 1st amendment

> Germany doesn't have a culture of celebrating the Nazis and how they were wronged and miss the "good ol days."

We rebuilt Germany to a better state than what it was. We didnt do that to the south

> Holocaust denial is outright illegal in Germany. You still have morons in the South still fully believing the Civil War was about state rights and not slavery.

Again, 1st amendment

> Germany has done nearly everything in its power to at least TRY (whether they're successful or not is an entirely different topic) to atone for the sins of their past. Not so in the US. 

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_anti-discrimination_acts#.C2.A0United_States

You are just wrong here

It seems like there's a lot of misunderstanding here. The comparison between the Confederacy and Nazi Germany isn't accurate. The treatment of both groups post-conflict is vastly different and shouldn't be equated. Let's focus on facts and not overgeneralize.

column7:
But here's the thing, you have to take it in the 1400 years ago context. Taking slaves -and torturing them- was a normal everyday part of that time. He encouraged on being kind and he actually dined with his slaves, which was a big thing back then. When you commit a bug sin in islam you can free slaves to make it better. So islam effectively abolished slavery or at least made it much less severe. 
When you say he plundered, they were tribesmen and they PLUNDERED, he introduced rules that are similar to today's war rules like not striking a man who isn't raising his sword, not striking women and not even cutting a tree down.
When you say raping children I assume you mean he married a young girl. Which was not only common back then but the norm.

Edit: like i said decapitating soldiers was the norm, he gave them a chance to be moslem, if they did they would not be decapitated.
He did not torture prisoners as far as I know, in fact he said be kind to them

Isn't it somewhat self-defeating to say that his actions should be judged on the context of the time in which he lived? If the time in which he lived is so radically different, then how relevant can his 1400 year old laws be today?

@diagonal-recon But isn't the point to evolve past those norms? We can't just keep saying "well everyone else was doing it too" when it comes to things like slavery and child marriage. Seems like we're justifying some pretty dark stuff there.

column7: No the thing is since i live in a place with many cultures the line have become blurry to me, and as someone who as been called both just white and half white because people have different definition to what white is. I need to know where do we even draw the line between races? I agree we have different ethinicities but to dive us into 5 big races just seems ignorant.

Race is this: 

>Race, as a social construct, is a group of people who share similar and distinct physical characteristics.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Race_(human_categorization)

That's really all it is. It's a word that means someone is noticeably different from one another. 

One important reason we categorize people by race is for medical conditions unique to, or more prevalent in some groups of people. People of African heritage are more likely to get sickle cell anemia, etc. 

There are many reasons race is important in the study of Anthropology, and in evolutionary biology. We can study characteristics of different races and come to conclusions about conditions that population evolved in, and help understand the historic movement of people through migration. 

Race is also important to people psychologically. Many people feel their history is part of their identity, and find a sense of camaraderie and fulfillment from the specific history of their evolutionary homeland. 

I think living in a fairly monocultural society like Sweden has been a factor in why you question the way people identify according to race. I grew up in a place that was statistically quite diverse, but my quadrant of the city was, like sweden, about 87% white.

Since then I've lived in Toronto. 

Toronto is roughly 50% foreign born citizens, and I believe they once determined the percentage of countries represented in Toronto... it was something like 85% of them. 

The approach there is to emphasize and celebrate the racial differences between populations, and leverage that as a way to create cultural depth for the city, and small pockets of communities innovating in their own cultural context. 

In short, using people's unique cultures, something we can't reproduce synthetically, to improve material and intangible quality of life. It works great for many places globally.

column7: Understood. You will just add any barrier to the scenario that helps prove your point. I concede that I don't stand a chance in the world in your head. I only have a chance out here in the real world.

>Why can't I open a competing shop to serve that other 50%?

You don't have enough capital. Banks who might loan you capital either do not want black people to be served or think that your shop has very little chance of survival against a shop that has a monopoly in the area. Other people who have enough capital either don't care enough about black people to invest their money in a risky business venture, don't want black people served, or see better investment opportunities. Many people who you could employ to work in your shop do not want to work in a "black neighbourhood." You cannot find a supplier who will provide you with goods at a low enough price that you can survive as a business. Racist people who don't want black people being served vandalise and rob your shop understanding that if you cannot make it a viable business within a certain amount of time it will have to close.

Add on top of that the hundreds of other problems faced by new businesses trying to compete with a monopoly regardless of racism issues.

column7:
Am I a bigot for wanting to leave a Tinder date if the girl shows up to it and she's 50 lbs heavier than I expected? Does that make me bigoted towards fat people? I mean, clearly I was attracted to her enough to want to go on a date with her in the first place. And the sole cause of me changing my mind is because she's fat and not skinny. So am I now a bigot? Or do I simply have certain sexual preferences?

Also, am I a homophobe for knowing I will never ever want to have sex with a man? Is every straight cis male a bigoted piece of shit? Or are we now trying to force people to act in a way that's counter to their natural (and harmless) sexual preferences?

I think it's extremely hypocritical for a trans person to say "You have to accept my autonomy and personal choices because I'm a human and I'm not hurting one by exercising that autonomy" but for them to then go "I refuse to accept that you're not sexually attracted to anyone but biological females with XX chromosomes, you have to change the way you live your life even though you're a also human and you're also not hurting anyone."

My sexual preference hurts *no one*. Even if someone says "I don't date black girls because I find them unattractive", that's not racist - it hurts nobody. That's having a sexual preference. Saying "I'm not attracted to black girls because they're lazy" or "I'm not attracted to black girls because they smell", *that's* racist because it plays into racial stereotypes. Simply not being attractive to certain features that are predominately exhibited by certain a race isn't racist, it's just nature. If someone is *only* attracted to pale skin, naturally they're not going to be attracted to people with anything but pale skin. Meaning, they're not going to be attracted to tan white girls the same way they won't be attracted to black girls. It's not racist, it's just nature - and it's wrong to force someone to feel sexual attraction for a group of people they have no natural sexual attraction to.

It's when the lack of attraction is rooted in bigoted, prejudicial conceptions that it becomes a problem. It's not prejudicial to say "I'm not attracted to trans people because I'm not attracted to people with Y chromosomes". It's prejudicial to say "I'm not attracted to trans people because they're weirdo sexual deviants." 

But to say I'm a bigot because I refuse to date anyone who has a Y chromosome only serves to alienate me against the LGBT cause. I'm 100% in favor of LGBT rights and think they deserve all the rights and freedoms every other American deserves. But as soon as they start showing a refusal to try to understand *my* lifestyle and *my* personal choices, well that's when I stop trying to understand their lifestyle and their personal choices. If neither of us are hurting anyone, then why should one of us get to judge the other as being wrong?

There's no way you can cut it and not be transphobic.  If a person is passing well and you'd have sex with them (IE you are attracted to them) but that stops when you are told they are Trans,  you are at least somewhat transphobic. How could it be any other way? If a friend came to you and said that he broke up with his seemingly white girlfriend because he found out she was a quarter black,  wouldn't you think  they are at least somewhat racist?  They must have some underlying factor beyond attraction if they found them acceptable before.

@chilly-inlay So, you're saying if someone's sexual preference changes based on new information, they're automatically bigoted? Seems like a pretty harsh judgment. What about the idea that people can have legitimate reasons for their preferences without being bigoted? Just because I prefer certain things doesn't mean I hate everything else. Isn't it possible to have preferences without being a hater?

"@spicy-staff Please keep discussions respectful and avoid using language that could be considered derogatory or offensive. Let's focus on constructive dialogue. @chilly-inlay and @archaic-pique Let's maintain a respectful tone and avoid assumptions about others' motivations. Keep the conversation productive."

column7: I believe that the right is causing the problem. yes, the center democrats (the majority of them) are largely reactive to the changes made by the right. as a result, the right is the one causing the problem. the right is changing the discourse, the centrist democrats are responding to those changes. therefore the centrist democrats arent the problem, they are reactionaries. Clinton, for example, did nothing that G.W. Bush wouldnt be proud of. But the right treated Clinton like he was satan himself. This is evidence that the right are the ones driving the breakdown in discourse.

So I think you have identified an important issue in that the language of the political parties are effectively different and hence the conversations aren't getting across meaning. But then a rational question to ask is, has the language of the left also not equally changed?

To me the answer to this is an obvious yes. Universities have introduced new ideas into the sphere that have aimed to be politically correct and remove normative terminology. They try to make language gender neutral, gender as a public concept has split from sex. Saying something insulting is equated as invalidating someone's existance, the concept of safe has been expanded to include mental safety. The list goes on. Both sides have changed languages.

The problem is during conversation we forget that the others sides terms don't mean what they mean to us. 

What's even worse is we attribute emotion to the difference of languages that effectively makes one side right and tbe other side wrong.

Look up Russell conjunction or an emotive conjunction.  Our language is changing, and the left seems to be applying the tenants of "new speak" from. 1984 and the right seems to be doing something wholly their own.

Laying the blame on one side of the spectrum, particularly one individual is exactly the type of division that this language has created.

@overcast-pole @worn-cataract So basically everyone’s just talking past each other and it’s all a big mess? Got it. But hey, at least we’re all feeling really strongly about it, right? /s

Maybe we should all just agree to disagree and stop trying to solve world problems on a forum. Who knew politics was so complicated? Not me!
